settings.title: Three Small Changes for Better Coffee at Home
settings.excerpt: You do not need a new machine to brew a noticeably better cup, just a few habits worth keeping.
settings.body:
People often ask how to make their home coffee taste more like the bar. The honest answer is that the small things matter most. Here are the three we would start with.

Grind just before you brew. Coffee goes stale quickly once it is ground. A burr grinder and a fresh grind will do more for your cup than almost any upgrade.

Weigh your coffee. A cheap kitchen scale takes the guesswork out. We aim for roughly 60 grams of coffee per litre of water, then adjust to taste.

Mind the water. Off the boil by thirty seconds is plenty. Fully boiling water scorches the grounds and pushes out a bitter edge.

Why these three come first

None of them cost much, and together they fix the problems we see most often. Stale grounds taste flat no matter how careful the rest of your method is. Guessing at the dose means every cup is a little different, so you never know what to change. And water that is too hot strips out harshness before the sweeter notes have a chance to come through. Get these right and a modest setup will outperform an expensive one used carelessly.

If you want a simple checklist to tape inside a cupboard, this is the order we would follow each morning:

  • Grind only what you are about to brew, never the night before.
  • Weigh both the coffee and the water, not just one of them.
  • Let the kettle sit off the boil for a slow count to thirty.

One more habit helps more than people expect: keep your beans in a sealed, opaque tin away from the stove and the window. Light, heat, and air are what turn a good bag dull within a week. A cool, dark shelf buys you days of freshness for nothing.

Change one thing at a time and taste the difference before adding the next. That way you learn what each habit actually does for your cup, and you can stop fiddling once it tastes the way you want.
